title: CAGER: Causal Geometric Explanation Recovery - A Framework for Grounding Interpretability in Causal Subspace Geometry
description: We propose CAGER (Causal Geometric Explanation Recovery), a framework that evaluates interpretability methods by measuring how well they recover the geometric structure of causally-grounded subspaces in neural networks. Unlike prior geometric evaluation approaches (e.g., Hedström et al.'s GEF), CAGER directly assesses whether methods capture geometric relationships among subspaces that are validated to be truly causal (not illusory), using a novel C-GAS metric with explicit safeguards against the interpretability illusion identified by Makelov et al.
motivation: Current interpretability evaluation lacks ground truth, relying on subjective human judgment or proxy metrics like reconstruction error. Meanwhile, recent work has shown that naive activation patching can produce 'interpretability illusions' where subspaces appear causal but actually activate dormant parallel pathways. There is a need for quantitative, causal-grounded evaluation that distinguishes true causal features from spurious ones. Our work fills this gap by directly measuring whether interpretability methods (like SAEs) recover the geometric structure of validated causal subspaces.
proposed_approach: CAGER operates in three stages: (1) Causal subspace identification using activation patching with multi-method validation to avoid illusions; (2) Mapping representations to explanation features using the method under evaluation; (3) Computing C-GAS (Causal Geometric Alignment Score), defined as the ratio of correlation between causal-explanation distances to correlation between full-explanation distances. The ratio formulation accounts for how much causal structure exists in the full space, providing a normalized measure of causal recovery. We use a threshold of ≥0.75 based on statistical significance (p<0.001), effect size guidelines, and empirical calibration on synthetic tasks.
key_innovations: Direct evaluation against causally-grounded subspaces rather than perturbation distortions
Multi-method validation (pathway consistency, ablation consistency, gradient agreement) to avoid interpretability illusions
C-GAS ratio metric that normalizes by the amount of causal structure present in the full representation
Clear distinction from Hedström et al.'s GEF: we measure recovery of causal subspaces, not alignment of explanation/model distortions

related_work.title: Evaluating Interpretable Methods via Geometric Alignment of Functional Distortions
related_work.authors: Anna Hedström, Philine Lou Bommer, Thomas F Burns, Sebastian Lapuschkin, Wojciech Samek, Marina M.-C. Höhne
related_work.distinction: Hedström et al. propose GEF, which measures alignment between explanation distortions and model output distortions under parameter perturbation. CAGER differs: (1) We evaluate recovery of causal subspace geometry, not distortion alignment; (2) We use ground-truth causal subspaces (with validation), while GEF is ground-truth-free; (3) We focus on feature-discovery methods with causal safeguards, while GEF targets general interpretability methods.

related_work.title: Is This the Subspace You Are Looking For? An Interpretability Illusion for Subspace Activation Patching
related_work.authors: Aleksandar Makelov, Georg Lange, Neel Nanda
related_work.distinction: Makelov et al. demonstrate that activation patching can identify spurious 'causal' subspaces that activate dormant parallel pathways. Our work directly addresses this by incorporating their recommended safeguards: multi-method validation, consistency checks across inputs, and gradient agreement tests. We cite this as a foundational challenge that our framework solves.

related_work.title: RAVEL: Evaluating Interpretability Methods on Disentangling Language Model Representations
related_work.authors: Jing Huang, Zhengxuan Wu, Chris Potts, Mor Geva, Atticus Geiger
related_work.distinction: Huang et al. introduce RAVEL, a benchmark for evaluating how well methods disentangle entity attributes. Their Cause and Iso metrics evaluate causal intervention success. We use RAVEL as one of our evaluation tasks, but our C-GAS metric differs by measuring geometric alignment of subspace structures rather than intervention success rates.

related_work.title: Towards Monosemanticity: Decomposing Language Models with Dictionary Learning
related_work.authors: Trenton Bricken, Adly Templeton, Joshua Batson, Brian Chen, Adam Jermyn, Tom Conerly, Nick Turner, Cem Anil, Carson Denison, Amanda Askell, Yuntao Bai, Anna Chen, Dawn Drain, Nelson Elhage, Danny Hernandez, Andy Jones, Jackson Kernion, Liane Lovitt, Zac Hatfield-Dodds, Kamal Ndousse, Catherine Olsson, Dario Amodei, Tom Brown, Jack Clark, Sam McCandlish, Chris Olah
related_work.distinction: Bricken et al. popularized SAEs for feature discovery. Our work evaluates whether SAEs (and similar methods) actually recover causally relevant features, addressing a key limitation noted in their work: lack of ground truth for evaluation.

related_work.title: Sparse Autoencoders Find Highly Interpretable Features in Language Models
related_work.authors: Hoagy Cunningham, Aidan Ewart, Logan Riggs, Robert Huben, Lee Sharkey
related_work.distinction: Cunningham et al. demonstrate SAEs can find interpretable features in language models. Our framework provides a principled way to evaluate whether these features are causally relevant, going beyond interpretability to establish causal grounding.

related_work.title: Toy Models of Superposition
related_work.authors: Nelson Elhage, Tristan Hume, Catherine Olsson, Nicholas Schiefer, Tom Henighan, Shauna Kravec, Zac Hatfield-Dodds, Robert Lasenby, Dawn Drain, Carol Chen, Roger Grosse, Sam McCandlish, Jared Kaplan, Dario Amodei, Martin Wattenberg, Christopher Olah
related_work.distinction: Elhage et al. explain polysemanticity through superposition. Our work builds on this by assuming features exist in linear subspaces that can be recovered by interpretability methods, and evaluating how well methods actually recover these features.

hypothesis: Interpretability methods that achieve higher C-GAS (Causal Geometric Alignment Score) on validated causal subspaces will generalize better to unseen examples and be more robust to the interpretability illusion. Specifically: (1) SAEs will achieve significantly higher C-GAS than baselines (PCA, random projections); (2) C-GAS with multi-method validation will correlate better with ground-truth causal features than C-GAS without validation; (3) C-GAS ≥ 0.75 will predict successful causal feature identification in >90% of cases.

confirmation: SAEs achieve C-GAS > 0.75 on validated causal subspaces, significantly higher than baselines (p < 0.01)
C-GAS correlates with ground-truth feature recovery rate (r > 0.8) on synthetic tasks
C-GAS with validation predicts generalization to steering/editing tasks better than C-GAS without validation
Illusory subspaces (failing validation) achieve significantly lower C-GAS than validated causal subspaces
refutation: No significant difference in C-GAS between SAEs and baselines (suggests metric lacks discriminative power)
C-GAS does not correlate with ground-truth recovery on synthetic tasks
Validation provides no improvement in C-GAS predictive power (suggests interpretability illusion is not significant)

experimental_plan_summary: Experiments on GPT-2 Small and Pythia models across three tasks: (1) Synthetic ground-truth validation with known causal features; (2) Indirect Object Identification (IOI) with known circuit structure; (3) Factual recall using RAVEL dataset. We evaluate SAEs (1×, 4×, 16× overcomplete), PCA, and random baselines. Each causal subspace undergoes multi-method validation (pathway consistency, ablation consistency, gradient agreement). Total compute: 6-8 hours on single A6000 GPU.

computational_requirements.evaluation: Causal identification: 30 min/task; Validation: 20 min/subspace set; Total: 6-8 hours

naming_justification.reason: Renamed from 'GEF' to avoid confusion with Hedström et al. (2025), who already use 'GEF' for 'Geometric Alignment of Functional Distortions'. CAGER emphasizes: (C)ausal grounding with validation safeguards, (A)lignment of (G)eometric structures, (E)xplanation recovery from model representations. The name is distinct and descriptive.
naming_justification.metric_reason: Changed from 'GAS' to 'C-GAS' to emphasize causal focus and avoid confusion with any existing metrics. The 'C-' prefix denotes 'Causal' and creates a unique identifier.
